author: @lisakingscott7729
text: I've been looking at readily available household chemicals to do this. Some descalers use formic acid, but often mixed with other things. Citric acid, acetic acid and lactic acid are very readily available. I'm sure you'll have a view on these Rob!

author: @michaelflood5710
text:
Great video Robert - A couple of questions: 

1) why copper formate specifically? Is there a more easily attainable form of copper that you can use for this? 
2) Is there an ideal ratio of copper formate to triethanolamine to use? Just molar equivalents?
3) Can you just air dry the substrate after painting the ink on, or does the triethanolamine force you to use some sort of active drying method? 

Again, really good stuff - thanks for making such excellent content.

author: @htomerif
text:
Any idea what the minimum trace width and space you can achieve is or how resistant to pulling free it is?
I'm gonna guess the minimum trace width is pretty bad, but I'd also guess that if you did the lasering of the copper formate/triethanolamine in a liquid that neither were soluble in it would keep that heating more localized and you'd get finer traces.

author: @drgazter
text:
Ok, I have several questions:
1.) Can you solder to the traces ?
2.) Can you flow the traces with solder ? (like cover everything in solder)
3.) Can you do multiple copper platings over each others to make thick copper traces ?
4.) How thick can the paint really be before the laser fails to penetrate it ?
5.) Does annealing the deposited micropolycrystalline copper traces improve conductivity ?
6.) Can we use something like silver formate to achieve similar results ?

author: @DanielXStaub
text: ​ @Robert Murray-Smith  I so want to try your version of this.  Please correct me anywhere I'm wrong, my last use of my college chemistry was over 20 years ago.  You take 'Copper formate tetrahydrate' and add Triethanolamine (TEA) (50 - 50 mix?).  It will turn to a rich deep blue color.  Paint it on the glass you want the copper on.  Let it dry.  You get a wonderful deep blue color that shouldn't be a bunch of small crystals.  Hit it with a laser and wash off the rest.  That leaves you with copper on any non-flexible material that can take the heat from the laser.  My laser will not be here until the end of next week so I want to try and make up some of this ink/paint so I can play around with it.  Thank you for posting this!

author: @lizardmech
text: Hey Rob, any idea what sort of conductivity you can get out of it? It might be possible to use this for making small multilayer thick film type circuits on ceramics. If you came up with a suitable dielectric that can be layered over it then selectively ablated by the laser to form interconnects then plated over with more copper you would have a means to create complex multilayer circuits. Ceramic thick film circuits are great for power electronics due to the high thermal conductivity and low thermal expansion. You can probably use your carbon ink to create resistors in the circuit in a similar way thick film circuits do as well. You could probably easily sell combinations of copper ink, dielectric and carbon as kits for making circuits on ceramics with a laser if it works.

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: @paulmaydaynight9925 lasering the Kapton tape makes graphene foam apparently (used a 405nm 50mW laser to make a couple small traces and tested for the conductivity, successfully, not sure yet on 455nm blue laser but I am going to try it too,) reasoning apparently is that the kapton tape is less likely to oxidize away unlike say styrene or some other carbon based plastics for the most part, (electrical current through a solution too of copper acetate solution I found creates quite a dense tough thick layer of copper metal, when trying to use EDM graphite electrodes to make contact with solution and aluminum on mylar *thermal blanket* to remove the aluminum and react it faster with the Cu Acetate solution instead of the copper just forming copper oxide, LOL)

author: @paulmaydaynight9925
text:
doped with triethanolamine v citric acid ?
c.o 
"Johann Swart on Breaking Taps thread

4 days ago
Great! I wonder how 405 nm will do. They are not DPSS, so their beam quality is much better, and the energy at those short wavelengths is quite a bit higher. Also, you could try citric acid in your last reaction. Its a great chelating agent for copper. Hopefully it will give it up when needed. All the best!"

Chelating agents are chemical compounds that react with metal ions to form a stable, water-soluble complex. They are also known as chelants, chelators, or sequestering agents. Chelating agents have a ring-like center which forms at least two bonds with the metal ion allowing it to be excreted. ?

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
In the paper mentioned by Robert,  in short,  technically no. I'm not sure what's going on in this video. 

Long answer is in the paper, the laser creates local hot spot. The heat changes the electical rest potential.  So what develops is an electrical potential that causes current to flow causing electrolytic plating.  
please forgive me if it sounds rather strange to point out that A causing B which causes C  is different than A causes C, which A does not, at least not with out B.  

The heat from the laser is not enough to reduce the metal ions to  metal.

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text: @kreynolds1123 apparently the light is oc , it's an 'And' thing here... btw good call on the "electrical potential" localised electrolytic plating part.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: @paulmaydaynight9925 what is oc?

replies.author: @paulmaydaynight9925
replies.text: @kreynolds1123 oc of course
